context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on pset 3 tbh, esp the sorting by reversals one. can u just explain that whole problem from the start?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But let's not start from scratch together; that's your job. 😊

Let's focus. Which specific part of Problem 1 is tripping you up — (a), (b), (c), or (d)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the problem, which would be giving away too much scaffolding. Instead, I should orient them by asking what specifically they find confusing, and narrow down to a specific part of the problem so we can work through it together using the Socratic method. I should not explain the whole problem from scratch.

exchanges.student: uh maybe all of it lol, but part (c) is frying my brain rn. i also don't really get how (a) and (b) are supposed to look either.
exchanges.tutor:
Let's build up to (c) by making sure (a) and (b) are solid first — they're shorter and will help a lot.

For part (a): a state machine needs states, transitions, and a start state. The problem already tells you what the states are. So what do you think the **transitions** should represent in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed but has identified part (c) as the main difficulty. I should start with (a) since it builds the foundation for everything else, and ask a simple targeted question to get them thinking. I'll use Socratic questioning to guide them without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: i guess like doing one reversal move? but then i'm not sure if it's any reversal or only the "good" ones fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Re-read the problem statement carefully — it actually tells you which reversals count as transitions. What does it say about that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track about transitions being reversal moves. The problem statement actually specifies this - transitions are good reversals. I should prompt them to re-read the problem statement carefully rather than just confirming their answer.

exchanges.student: i think only good reversals, but then i'm still confused abt the rest of the setup ngl. can u just say what the full state machine is supposed to be?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got it — transitions are good reversals. That's correct!

Now you just need one more piece for (a): what's the **start state**?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the full answer, which I should not provide. They've actually correctly identified that transitions are good reversals. I should affirm that and push them to complete part (a) themselves by asking about the start state, which is the remaining piece.

exchanges.student: maybe the given permutation \((x_0,\dots,x_{n-1})\)? tbh i'm still not seeing the whole picture tho.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right! You actually have all three pieces now — states, transitions, and start state. That's part (a) done. Try writing it up in your own words.

For part (b): what do you think the **final states** are, and how would you know when the machine has stopped?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all three components of the state machine for part (a): states are permutations, transitions are good reversals, and start state is the given permutation. I should affirm this and push them to write it up and move to part (b), rather than dwelling further on (a).

exchanges.student: i'm guessing when there are no good reversals left, but idk why that means it's sorted. that's the part that's messing me up.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're on the right track. Now think about it this way: a good reversal requires an **inverted pair**. So if no good reversals are possible, what does that tell you about inverted pairs in the list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that final states are when no good reversals remain. Now I need to guide them to see why no good reversals implies sorted. This connects directly to the definition of an inverted pair and a good reversal. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question about what 'no inverted pairs' means for the list.

exchanges.student: that there aren't any inverted pairs i think? but then part (c) still feels super random, like where does the base-n thing even come from.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly — no inverted pairs means sorted. That's your partial correctness argument for (b). Nice!

For (c): the hint says to think of the permutation as digits of a base-n number. If the permutation were fully sorted (0, 1, 2, ..., n−1), what would that number look like compared to any other permutation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly concluded that no inverted pairs means sorted - I should briefly affirm that and connect it to partial correctness, then move to (c). For (c), rather than explaining the hint, I should ask a Socratic question that helps them see why thinking of a permutation as a base-n number is useful - specifically, what property a sorted list has as a base-n number versus an unsorted one.

exchanges.student: idk, probably kinda small? i'm honestly lost again tho, can u pls just explain the base-n idea from scratch.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think! You said the sorted permutation would give a "small" number — can you be more precise? Is it the smallest possible base-n number you could form from those digits, or the largest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which I should not provide. However, their intuition that the sorted permutation gives a 'small' number is actually the key insight. I should push them to be more precise about this rather than explaining from scratch. The sorted permutation gives the smallest possible base-n number, and a good reversal should decrease this number - that's the derived variable.

exchanges.student: i think smallest, but i don't really know why reversing a good pair would always make that number go down.
exchanges.tutor: Good — smallest is right! Now focus on just the leftmost position that changes in a good reversal. If i < j and a_i > a_j, what happens to the digit at position i after the reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the sorted permutation gives the smallest base-n number. Now they need to understand why a good reversal decreases the base-n value. I should guide them to think about what a good reversal does to the most significant digit that changes - since i < j and a_i > a_j, the higher-value digit moves to a less significant position. I'll ask a targeted question about what happens to the leftmost changed position.

exchanges.student: it becomes \(a_j\), which is smaller i guess. but then a bunch of later digits change too, so i can't tell why that guarantees the whole base-\(n\) number decreases.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the key insight! Now think about magnitudes: in base-n, how large can the total change from positions i+1 through j possibly be, compared to a change of even 1 at position i?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the key insight - the most significant changed digit decreases. Now they need to realize that in base-n, the most significant digit dominates - no matter what happens to the less significant digits, a decrease at position i outweighs any possible increase at positions i+1 through j. I should guide them to think about the magnitude of the change at position i versus the maximum possible change in the remaining positions.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, never giving away answers directly. Each time the student asked for a full explanation, the tutor redirected with a targeted question. The tutor built progressively on student responses, acknowledged correct steps, and guided the student toward the base-n insight through incremental questioning. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the conversation stayed on-task. Responses were concise and appropriately supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never provided direct answers or near-submission-ready content; every response was a guiding question or brief affirmation followed by a next question. The tutor built on each correct student response (e.g., confirming 'good reversals' as transitions, 'no inverted pairs' as the termination condition, 'smallest' as the sorted base-n value) before advancing. When the student asked for full explanations, the tutor redirected with focused sub-questions rather than dumping information. Responses were bite-sized, tone was warm and encouraging without being sycophantic, and no grading language was used. The conversation progressed logically without repetitive cycling.
